title: SEGMENT 6 — The IND — your permission slip, and the thirty-day clock

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — The IND — your permission slip, and the thirty-day clock
So now you have done your preclinical work, you have had your pre-IND meeting, you have addressed the agency's feedback, and you are ready to file.
Welcome to the IND.
The Investigational New Drug application — IND — is the regulatory submission that gives you permission to begin clinical trials.
It is not, despite the name, an approval of the drug.
It is a permission to test the drug in humans.
The distinction matters.
The IND is a research permit, not a marketing authorization.
The drug itself remains unapproved throughout the entire clinical trial process.
You are conducting investigations under the IND.
You are not selling a product.
What is in an IND?
Let me give you the rough table of contents.
An IND has three big sections.

First, the chemistry, manufacturing, and controls section — usually called CMC — which describes how the drug is made, what its physical and chemical properties are, what tests you do to confirm it is what you say it is, and what your quality system looks like.
We are going to spend a whole segment on CMC later because it is often the limiting step for gene therapy.
Second, the preclinical pharmacology and toxicology section — which is the safety dossier we discussed in Segment 4, presented in the format the agency expects.

Third, the clinical protocol — the actual plan for the trial: who will be enrolled, how they will be dosed, what will be measured, what will count as a success or a failure, and what will trigger stopping the trial early.
Let me give you the analogy.
The IND is your permission slip.
When you were a kid in school and you wanted to go on a field trip, your teacher gave you a piece of paper to bring home, and your parents had to sign it and you had to bring it back the next day.
The signed paper was the permission slip.
Without it, you couldn't get on the bus.
The IND is the same thing, except the field trip is "first-in-human dosing of a brand new gene therapy" and the parent whose signature you need is the FDA.
But here is the twist.
The IND does not work by waiting for the agency to say yes.
It works on a clock.
You submit your IND.
You then wait thirty days.
If the agency does not put you on hold within thirty days, your IND is automatically active, and you can begin dosing patients.
Silence is consent.
The agency is not required to formally approve your IND.
They are required to act on it within thirty days if they have concerns.
If they have no concerns, or if they have concerns they are willing to address through ongoing dialogue rather than a formal hold, you simply start your trial on day thirty-one.
Now, in practice, almost every IND for a complex product like a gene therapy gets some kind of agency response within the thirty-day window.

The response might be a clinical hold — meaning you cannot dose patients until the agency lifts it — or it might be a letter listing concerns the agency wants you to address but that does not formally block the trial.
Sometimes you get partial holds, where you can start enrolling at a low dose but not escalate until specific issues are resolved.
The thirty-day clock is real, but it is not a clean binary; it is the structure within which a back-and-forth dialogue happens.
The most important thing the agency can do during that thirty-day window is put you on clinical hold.

The clinical hold is a particularly important regulatory mechanism, and I want you to understand it as a standing power that the agency has throughout the entire clinical life of a drug, not just at the start.
The FDA can place a clinical hold on your trial at any time.
Before you start dosing.
After you have dosed five patients.
In the middle of a Phase 2 study.
The day before you file your BLA.
If a serious adverse event occurs, or if new data emerges that calls the safety profile into question, the agency can require you to stop.
You cannot dose another patient until the hold is lifted.
The analogy here is straightforward.
The clinical hold is the emergency brake.
It is the cord on the train that anyone can pull.
The agency holds the cord throughout the entire ride.
Most of the time they don't pull it.
But the fact that they can pull it changes how sponsors behave.
You design your trial knowing that if a safety signal emerges, the agency can stop you.
You report adverse events promptly knowing that withholding could trigger a hold and a much bigger problem.
You build relationships with your CBER reviewers so that when an issue arises you can have a substantive conversation about it before they reach for the brake.
For gene therapy, clinical holds have been a particularly important feature of the regulatory landscape.
The field has had several high-profile holds — some after serious adverse events, including deaths, in specific trials — and each hold has reshaped how the field operates afterwards.
Holds in the AAV space following hepatotoxicity events at high systemic doses, for instance, led to new guidance on dose selection and on the structure of safety monitoring committees.
The hold is not just a punishment; it is a feedback mechanism that the field as a whole learns from, even when it is painful for the individual sponsor on the receiving end.
So that is the IND.
Permission slip.
Thirty-day clock.
Clinical hold as the standing emergency brake.
Get through the thirty days, address whatever response the agency sends back, and you are in clinical trials.
Which, of course, is when the real work begins.
